title: One sentence of policy: the exception and the ghost of Annex A
paras: One sentence of policy — the exception, and the ghost of Annex A. National policy carries the whole exception in a single provision. Decisions should avoid isolated homes in the countryside unless, among other circumstances, there's an essential need for a rural worker — including those taking majority control of a farm business — to live permanently at or near their place of work. That's paragraph 84 a of the N-P-P-F at the current edition — and the number has moved with successive revisions, so it should be checked every time. No functional test is set out in terms. No financial test, no three-year profitability requirement, and no definition of essential. The Framework states the exception and leaves the assessment to the authority — and that silence is the source of most of the difficulty in this field.

The detail lived elsewhere, and its lineage matters. D-o-E Circular 24 of 73 established the national policy for agricultural and forestry dwellings — functional need, tied to financial viability, with the standard occupancy condition. P-P-G 7 Annex E, in 1997, and then P-P-S 7 Annex A, in 2004, carried the approach forward, each more or less repeating the last — until the whole structure was swept away in March 2012, when the first Framework replaced around a thousand pages of guidance with one short document. The detail didn't survive the change. The expectations of decision-makers, in large part, did.

Annex A was a structured test, and its limbs are still the spine of every assessment. For a permanent dwelling on an established unit, it required a clearly established existing functional need. It required that the need related to a full-time worker. It required that the unit had been established for at least three years, had been profitable for at least one, and was currently financially sound with a clear prospect of remaining so. It required that the need couldn't be met by another dwelling on the unit, or by other suitable accommodation available in the area. And it required that ordinary planning requirements were satisfied. For new enterprises, it prescribed the temporary route — a caravan or readily dismantled structure while the business proved itself. Each limb became a battleground, and each survives in substance today.

The Framework's silence created a vacuum, and authorities filled it with the very guidance that was withdrawn. You'll find local plans adopted since 2012 that reproduce the old tests — giving them fresh statutory footing. You'll find supplementary planning documents lifting the wording more or less directly, a few authorities formally adopting Annex A as local guidance, and many applying it simply as habit. Meanwhile, the modern wording is wider in two respects the old guidance never was. First, "rural worker" reaches beyond agriculture and forestry to equestrian and other land-based enterprises. And second, the express reference to majority control names the succession case that previously had to be argued the hard way, through the general functional test.

title: Functional need: essential, not convenient
paras: Functional need — essential, not convenient. Everything turns on one question: is it essential for the proper functioning of the enterprise that a worker is present at or near the site? If yes, the application has a foundation. If no, nothing else can save it. The old guidance put the test in words that remain the working definition, even though the document carrying them is withdrawn. A functional test is necessary to establish whether it is essential, for the proper functioning of the enterprise, for one or more workers to be readily available at most times — as where animals or agricultural processes require essential care at short notice, or to deal quickly with emergencies that could otherwise cause serious loss, such as frost damage or the failure of automatic systems. That formulation hasn't been improved upon.

Two phrases carry most of the weight. Readily available at most times isn't met by showing a worker is busy, works long hours, or is rarely off the holding — a nine-to-five operation, however demanding, doesn't generate the need. At short notice means delay causes real harm to an animal, a crop or a process. Where the harm can wait until morning, the case for residence weakens. Hence the worked contrast at the heart of the assessment. A dairy herd is milked morning and night, calves across much of the year, and a cow in difficulty needs help within the hour — human presence alters the outcome. The arable farm next door grows unsupervised. Frost and hail do their damage whether or not anyone is present, and intensity at drilling and harvest is not the same as a need for a resident worker. The useful old phrase is that the worker must be within sight and sound of the enterprise — supervision that's real, not nominal — which is also why the functional case and the siting of the dwelling are connected.

The boundaries are precise. Welfare is the heart of the strongest livestock cases — calving, farrowing, lambing, stock that fall ill or become cast at any hour. But the weak version, that any farm with animals must have a dwelling, is rightly resisted: many livestock farms run without every worker resident, and fattening cattle have traditionally been finished in open yards without close supervision. Security contributes on remote holdings with genuine rural crime, but it's long been understood not to justify a dwelling on its own — though it may inform siting, because a dwelling close to the access controls the access. A need arising from processing or manufacturing, rather than the agricultural operation, doesn't support an agricultural dwelling. And the need belongs to the enterprise, not the applicant. It's established by the work, the stock, the land and the timing — and it would be the same need whoever did the work.

The need is assessed on the enterprise as it now operates. An established need supports a permanent dwelling; a prospective one — the herd still building, the venture still a plan — points to the temporary route. Change is the classic driver of a genuine need arising. Livestock introduced onto an arable holding — the Embleton situation. Expansion carrying the labour requirement across the full-time threshold. A change of system — outdoor stock brought in, breeding added to fattening. Or changed circumstances on an established holding — the owner who, through age or ill health, can no longer do work that still has to be done by someone. In every pattern, the change must have an agricultural purpose of its own, and it must actually have happened. Stock on the ground with numbers and dates, buildings erected and in use, records showing the new system in operation — not intentions.

title: Measuring the enterprise: the Standard Labour Requirement
paras: Measuring the enterprise — the Standard Labour Requirement. The functional test establishes whether an enterprise needs a worker present at all. The Standard Labour Requirement establishes whether it's big enough for that need to be real and substantial, rather than notional. The method runs in steps. List every class of stock by head, and every crop and grassland type by hectare — accurately, because the inputs must reconcile with the holding's records, accounts and subsidy returns. Multiply each by the published standard labour figure and total the hours, grassland included. The recognised datasets are the John Nix Farm Management Pocketbook — expressed in standard man-days — the S-A-C figures, and the Agricultural Budgeting and Costing Book, with the DEFRA Farm Business Survey figures available as an independent method.

Then add for overheads — typically 10 per cent, up to 20 per cent depending on the enterprises, applied once — and divide by the productive hours one full-time worker provides in a year. The divisor sits below contracted hours because of holidays, sickness and ordinary unproductive time. 2,200 hours is widely used — but know, and be able to justify, whatever divisor the appraisal has used, because a quietly low divisor reports more units for the same holding. Finally, run the calculation for each dataset and take a reasoned average. The datasets differ — one may assume a more intensive system, count tasks differently, or rest on older data — and the spread is information, not a flaw to hide.

The worked contrast shows why the figure is so powerful. Take a 365-acre dairy holding carrying 365 cows in two calving groups, with followers. It returns 10.27 units on the A-B-C figures, 8.22 on S-A-C, and 7.00 on Nix — a reasoned average of 8.50 Standard Labour Units, with the independent Farm Business Survey method returning almost exactly the same figure. That leaves an opponent very little room. Now take the identical 365 acres, cropped with winter wheat, winter barley and oilseed rape. It returns 0.55, 1.39 and 0.98 — an average of 0.97, not even one full-time job. Identical land, and a labour requirement differing by a factor of roughly nine, according to what's done with it. That's the difference between a case that writes itself and one with almost nothing to stand on.

Be clear what the figure means. It measures labour consumed, not houses justified. An S-L-R of eight doesn't mean eight dwellings, because much of the labour can travel in — the functional test, not the labour figure, governs how many workers must be resident. The working thresholds run like this. A first dwelling needs an S-L-R in excess of one. An S-L-R of two doesn't require two dwellings. And a second becomes arguable only as the S-L-R approaches or exceeds three, depending on the stock and enterprises. Alongside it, livestock units and stocking density — the dairy example carries some 424 L-S-U, at about 2.9 per hectare — corroborate that the stock counted can actually be carried on the land. That's the quick check against agents tempted to inflate stock numbers to drive the S-L-R up.

title: The financial position and the viability myth
paras: The financial position, and the viability myth. There is no financial viability test for a permanent rural worker's dwelling under the Framework — yet applicants still arrive with accounts as their lead evidence, and officers still demand them, because both are working from a test that no longer exists. Two questions get muddled: whether the work needs a worker on the land, and whether the farm turns a profit. Only the first is the test. A holding can show a clear need and run at break-even — or at a loss in a poor year — and still justify a dwelling. A profitable enterprise with no need will not. The habit is a leftover from P-P-S 7 Annex A, whose sound-financial-basis and economic-viability limbs were hard tests for years — and which were simply not carried into the Framework in 2012. The policy didn't soften the test, or move it. It dropped it.

The courts were uneasy with mechanical financial tests even before 2012. In Petter versus S-S-E-T-R, reported at 2000, 79 P-and-C-R 214, the Court of Appeal quashed a refusal of a temporary permission on a seventeen-acre subsistence smallholding, where the inspector — having accepted the functional need — refused because net profit fell below the agricultural minimum wage. The financial test existed to stop bogus applications, and to give assurance that the farming — and so the reason for the house — would continue. It was a means to that end, not an end in itself. For a holding run on a subsistence basis, profitability was no guide to whether it was genuine, and a poor guide to whether it would last. Where money is still in issue, Petter is the authority against a crude profit test.

The Framework question was answered in Embleton — that's R, on the application of Embleton Parish Council, versus Northumberland County Council, reported at 2013, E-W-H-C 3631, Admin. A 173-hectare arable holding, a calf-rearing enterprise moving on, a temporary caravan — and a challenge arguing that viability was implicit in the isolated-homes paragraph. The judge rejected it. The N-P-P-F test differs from Annex A, and simply requires a judgment of whether the enterprise has an essential need for a worker to be there, or near there. Notably, even the council's own consultant put it no higher than planning on a sound financial basis — the old temporary-dwelling wording. Three cautions temper the weight. It's a deputy High Court decision. It arose on a temporary dwelling, and the judge leaned on that context. And its reasoning sits in some tension with Redhill Aerodrome, reported at 2014, E-W-C-A Civ 1386, where the Court of Appeal declined to read N-P-P-F brevity as silent abandonment of predecessor policy. Embleton has not been displaced, and inspectors follow it consistently — but it's not bulletproof.

Money still matters twice over. First, financial evidence goes to whether the farming is real and will continue: stock and cropping records, sale receipts, subsidy claims, accounts as proof of an operating business — rather than a profit return to be marked pass or fail. And investment in new buildings, services, accesses or fencing shows a financial commitment to the enterprise that shouldn't be discounted. The sound-financial-basis expectation survives in one corner: temporary dwellings for new enterprises, where a business plan is still looked for, because a venture with no track record can only be judged by how it was planned. Second, a local plan financial test is part of the development plan under section 38, subsection 6 — and it's not a dead letter. The argument runs in sequence. The plan has primacy. Embleton supplies the material consideration that national policy contains no viability test. And City of Edinburgh, reported at 1997, 1 W-L-R 1447, together with Hopkins Homes, reported at 2017, U-K-S-C 37, supply the principle that a policy outdated by a change in national policy may be given reduced weight. The submission is that little weight attaches — never that the policy is void.

title: Suitable and available accommodation
paras: Suitable and available accommodation. Functional need gets an application to the starting line — this question defeats more of them than any other. If a suitable dwelling already exists, or is available nearby, the essential need is met without building, and the justification falls away. The limbs are separate, and each must be made out. Suitable means fit to meet the particular established need. A dwelling miles from the stock isn't suitable, whatever its condition. Nor is one too small for the worker's household, or so expensive to run that it's beyond an agricultural wage, or so placed that it can't give the close supervision the enterprise requires. Available means available in fact, not merely in existence. A house occupied by someone else, not for sale or to let, or priced beyond the reach of the worker who must live in it, isn't available in any real sense. Running the limbs together is a common error — and it cuts both ways.

The leading case is Keen versus S-S-E, reported at 1996, 2 P-L-R 8. The need for a resident stockman wasn't in dispute. The refusal rested on the applicant's own substantial four-bedroom family home nearby, which the inspector said could be made available, or shared, at the critical times of year — accepting this might cause substantial inconvenience and financial loss. The court quashed the decision. Accommodation may exist in fact, but its availability and suitability must be subjected to scrutiny — mere existence cannot suffice. The questions the inspector should have asked remain the checklist. Was the house actually available as a matter of fact? What other demands were on it? Could the applicant really be expected to leave his home and buy another for his family? How would sharing work if the stockman were single, married, or married with children? Did the house lend itself to sharing, or adaptation, at all? An occupier who has established a need can't reasonably be expected to give up, or carve up, his own home.

Cussons shows the principle worked through — that's J-R Cussons and Son versus S-S-C-L-G, reported at 2008, E-W-H-C 443, Admin. Three farms, around 130 suckler cows, calving spread across October to May, with near-continuous observation evidenced by a vet's letter. The inspector accepted an additional worker might be needed, but pointed to three alternatives — and each was quashed for the same defect. The large house at Bridge Farm "could" accommodate the worker. Asserted, not tested — and Mr Cussons senior, after a double heart bypass, could no longer attend stock through the night, so the proximity that once made it workable no longer held. And converting it into two dwellings would itself need permission. The appeal building "could" provide temporary accommodation — against months of calving, how temporary? The vacant farmhouse "might" be rented or bought — from an owner the applicant didn't get on with, who might neither sell nor let. A possibility is not availability. An alternative that exists only after works, consents, or someone else's cooperation is not, without more, an available one.

Three scenarios recur. First, the dwelling sold away from the holding — the severance problem. The authority fairly asks why a new house should replace one voluntarily given up, but the answer lies in the same two limbs. The sold-off house is almost always in separate ownership, occupied, and not for sale — and often no longer suitable, having been extended, gentrified, or severed from the buildings. The closer the sale sits to the application, the sharper the scrutiny. Second, the dwelling already tied by an occupancy condition. It defeats the application only if it's suitable and available to the present need. A tied house lawfully occupied by another agricultural worker, or a retired one, isn't available on demand, and one tied cottage doesn't cap a holding whose need has genuinely grown. Conversely, if the tied house is suitable and available, the new application shouldn't be made. And third, houses on the open market. They're usually unsuitable by distance — a village house two miles away can't serve a need defined by sight and sound — and unavailable by price, since a house at open-market value isn't available to a worker on an agricultural wage. That's part of why the tied dwelling the policy contemplates is a modest one.

title: Temporary dwellings and the occupancy tie
paras: Temporary dwellings, and the occupancy tie. A new or expanding enterprise whose need is prospective takes the temporary route: typically a caravan or mobile home for three years, modest and removable by design — so that if the case is never made out, the structure comes off and little harm is done. Three things carry the temporary application. First, a credible prospect that the functional need will arise, shown by a projected labour requirement drawn from the recognised datasets, with the same rigour as a present one. Second, a firm intention and reasonable likelihood that the enterprise will develop as planned — the surviving role of the business plan, distinguishing a genuine farming proposal from an aspirational lifestyle project. And third, the absence of suitable available accommodation — tested at the temporary stage, not deferred. What the period now tests is permanence rather than viability: whether the need, once it arises, is genuine, established and enduring. Land tenure feeds that judgment. A wholly owned holding reads as permanent. One whose core is owned, but which leans on farm business tenancies, less so. And a steading dependent on annually licensed land least of all — because if the land goes, the stock go, and the need evaporates.

The period ends in one of three ways. The need has become established — the stock are on the ground, the labour requirement has crossed the threshold — and the permanent application is judged on the position as it stands, with the functional test applied afresh, and more demandingly, since a temporary consent is not a promise of a permanent one. Or the need hasn't become established, and the accommodation must be removed and the land restored. That's the bargain the consent represents. Or the position is unresolved, and a further period is sought — and temporary permissions are not freely renewable. Repeated extensions were treated as unsatisfactory as long ago as Petter, because an unending series of temporary consents would collapse the distinction between temporary and permanent, and secure permanent occupation by the back door. An applicant at the end of three years, still relying on what the enterprise will become, has usually run out of road.

A permitted dwelling carries the occupancy condition. The standard form, essentially unchanged since 1973, limits occupation to a person solely or mainly employed, or last employed, in agriculture or forestry in the locality — together with a widow, widower or surviving civil partner of such a person, and resident dependants. Note what it doesn't say. It doesn't tie occupation to the particular holding. A worker on another farm in the area, a local agricultural contractor, or a retired worker all qualify — which gives owners more scope to find an occupier than is assumed, and weakens the removal argument that none could be found. The suppressed value isn't a side effect but the function: the condition keeps the dwelling affordable to the workers it exists to house, and the difference between tied and untied value — often a substantial proportion of the open-market figure — is what every removal contest is really about. Survivor protection is personal. When the surviving partner dies or leaves, the condition revives in full.

Removal runs by two routes. The first is by application — in practice under section 73 of the Town and Country Planning Act 1990, producing a fresh permission judged against current policy — on the ground that the condition is no longer necessary. That's proved above all by marketing: the dwelling genuinely and visibly offered to qualifying occupiers, through channels they'd actually see — the agricultural press, specialist agents, the obvious websites — at a price reflecting the tie, where the discount is a matter of valuation evidence, with figures in the region of a third often cited — and for a sustained period, commonly in the order of twelve months, with no qualifying interest. Arguing only that the original holding has gone fails on the wording: the tie is to agriculture in the locality, so the argument comes back to marketing across the locality. The second route is by time. Ten years' continuous breach by a non-qualifying occupier confers immunity, confirmed by certificate. The burden is the owner's, on the balance of probabilities. Any period of compliant occupation — including by a qualifying survivor — resets the clock. And the ten-year period for breach of condition was unaffected by the 2024 enforcement-time-limit reforms. Check the instrument first. Pre-1973 conditions carry non-standard wording that may be imprecise enough to challenge, and some ties are section 106 obligations — usually tying the dwelling to land rather than dictating occupants — with entirely different procedures for modification.
